risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomisation was conducted using pre-generated random permuted blocks (block sizes of four and six). A data manager at the York Trials Unit, who was completely independent of the research team, created the randomisation programme" Comment: The study reports pre-generated random permuted blocks with block sizes specified, which indicates a truly random allocation sequence was generated. Although the exact random-number method is not named, the described permuted-block randomisation is sufficient to judge low risk for sequence generation.

Quote: "After completing the baseline assessment, the research nurse telephoned a secure and remote randomisation service, located at the York Trials Unit (University of York, UK)." Comment: Allocation was concealed by central randomisation via a secure remote service, which prevented the enrolling nurse from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Since assessment of wound healing is somewhat subjective and staff and participants could not be blinded to treatment" and "Participants commenced their allocated treatment immediately following randomisation." Comment: The paper explicitly states that participants and staff could not be blinded to treatment allocation. Because the key outcomes included wound healing, pain, and adverse events—outcomes plausibly influenced by awareness of allocation and treatment—this creates a high risk of performance bias overall.

Quote: "A blinded outcome assessor (experienced Tissue Viability nurse not involved in the day-to-day conduct of the trial) then assessed photographs for (1) assessment of healing, (2) threats to blinding and (3) picture quality." Comment: The outcome assessor was explicitly blinded, and the key healing outcome was assessed from digital photographs rather than at the bedside. Although wound healing can be somewhat subjective, the study used a blinded independent assessor for photographic review, making detection bias for healing unlikely.

Quote: "There was no attrition recorded during this trial. From the 12 recruited participants, 5 ... exited the trial after completing the maximum 6-month follow-up. A further five participants ... completed less than the 6-month follow-up as they were recruited later into the study. Two participants died during the trial..." Comment: The report explicitly states no attrition/loss to follow-up occurred; all participants were accounted for, and the shorter follow-up in some cases was due to late recruitment rather than missing data. This indicates essentially complete outcome data with no clear attrition-related risk of bias.
